summary: Machine policy for the Application Telemetry engine used by application compatibility tracking. The local Microsoft AppCompat.admx file explicitly documents the same policy key, value name, and unusual 0/1 mapping that the app uses.

setting.name: Application Compatibility Telemetry
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can collect anonymous telemetry about how applications use certain system components so compatibility systems can learn from it. This policy decides whether that telemetry engine runs.
setting.why_people_change_it: People turn it off when they want less telemetry on the device or a tighter enterprise privacy baseline.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off reduces compatibility telemetry collection, but it also removes a signal Windows can use for compatibility analysis. Microsoft also notes that if CEIP is already off, this telemetry is off regardless of this policy.
setting.professional_notes: AppCompat.admx and the ADMX_AppCompat Policy CSP map AITEnable to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat\AITEnable with enabledValue 0 and disabledValue 1. In this policy, enabling the policy means turning telemetry off.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the Application Telemetry policy through this registry value.

allowed_values.label: Application Telemetry turned off
allowed_values.meaning: The Application Telemetry engine is turned off for the device.

allowed_values.label: Application Telemetry allowed
allowed_values.meaning: This policy does not turn the Application Telemetry engine off.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes AITEnable = 0, which matches the documented policy state that turns Application Telemetry off.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 7 and newer systems exposing the AppCompat telemetry policy

states.rationale: The policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: This is separate from the CEIP baseline. Microsoft states that if CEIP is turned off, Application Telemetry is off regardless of how this policy is set.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that do not need a hard application-telemetry policy
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its ordinary policy baseline for Application Telemetry.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You explicitly want compatibility telemetry turned off.
tradeoffs: No hard policy block is written.
Application Telemetry can still operate unless another policy such as CEIP disables it.

recommended_profiles.label: Privacy focused
intended_for: Systems that want less compatibility telemetry
avoid_for: People who want all Windows compatibility telemetry left available

states.rationale: Enable the policy that turns Application Telemetry off.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the Application Telemetry engine explicitly turned off.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want Windows compatibility telemetry left available.
tradeoffs: Anonymous Application Telemetry collection is turned off.

evidence.title: Microsoft ADMX_AppCompat Policy CSP: AppCompatTurnOffApplicationImpactTelemetry
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the policy that turns the Application Telemetry engine off, including the registry mapping.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppCompat.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps AITEnable under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat with enabledValue 0 and disabledValue 1.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppCompat.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text explains that enabling the policy turns Application Telemetry off and notes that telemetry is also off when CEIP is turned off.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes AITEnable = 0 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft AppCompat.admx file explicitly defines AppCompatTurnOffApplicationImpactTelemetry under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat with valueName AITEnable and the enabledValue 0 / disabledValue 1 mapping that the app uses.

validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same policy key and value name that the app writes and confirms the reversed 0/1 semantics for this policy.
